content.text: Reducing the numerical precision of a model's weights—a technique called quantization—is the primary lever that makes billion-parameter LLMs practical on consumer hardware. A 70-billion-parameter model stored in 16-bit floats requires roughly 140 GB of memory; the same model quantized to 4-bit integers fits in around 38 GB, enabling it to run on a single high-end workstation GPU. Every major local inference stack—llama.cpp, Ollama, vLLM, and TGI—relies on quantization formats at its core.

Quantization maps continuous floating-point weight values to a smaller set of discrete integer levels. A full-precision model stores each weight as a 32-bit float (FP32) or, more commonly after training, a 16-bit float (FP16 or BF16). Quantization replaces those with 8-bit integers (INT8), 4-bit integers (INT4), or even 2- to 3-bit representations, reducing both storage and the memory bandwidth required during inference.
The core idea is straightforward: given a tensor of weights
such that each weight can be approximately reconstructed as

is the quantized integer. The per-block scale parameters are stored alongside the quantized weights in formats such as GGUF, allowing the engine to dequantize on the fly during matrix multiplications.
There are two main categories of quantization.
applies compression to an already-trained model without any gradient updates. It is fast to apply but may lose accuracy on sensitive tasks.

simulates quantization noise during fine-tuning so the model learns to tolerate lower precision, but it requires access to a training pipeline and significant compute. Most local deployment scenarios rely on PTQ because the base model weights come pre-quantized from Hugging Face or TheBloke repositories.

Memory bandwidth is the dominant bottleneck during autoregressive token generation. Each forward pass must load every model weight from DRAM into registers. A 70B FP16 model loads 140 GB per token generated; with INT4 quantization that drops to 35 GB—a 4× speedup in memory-bound scenarios even before considering any arithmetic savings. For agentic systems that call the model hundreds of times per task, this throughput improvement compounds into dramatically lower latency budgets.

VRAM constraints are the practical gating factor. Consumer GPUs cap at 8–24 GB; professional cards reach 48–80 GB; multi-GPU configurations are expensive to operate. Quantization determines which model sizes are reachable on a given hardware budget. A practitioner with a single RTX 4090 (24 GB) cannot run a 13B FP16 model (26 GB) but can comfortably run it at Q4_K_M (7 GB) with marginal quality loss on most tasks.
— INT4 quantization cuts memory usage by roughly 4× versus FP16, enabling larger models to run on a given GPU.
— less data to transfer per forward pass directly translates to more tokens per second in memory-bandwidth-bound generation.
— smaller files load from disk into VRAM faster, reducing cold-start latency for agents.
— a quantized model running on one GPU replaces a multi-GPU setup, cutting hardware and energy costs.
— running a quantized model locally means sensitive data never leaves the machine, which is essential for regulated industries.
— llama.cpp can offload layers that do not fit in VRAM to RAM, with quantized models making this practical by keeping the per-layer footprint small.

Quantization introduces quantization error, which is the difference between the original and reconstructed weight. In practice, LLMs are surprisingly tolerant of this noise—benchmark scores typically drop 1–3 perplexity points from FP16 to Q4_K_M—but the tolerance is not uniform across tasks or model components.
Tool-call and structured JSON accuracy degrades first at aggressive quants; function-calling agents require quality testing before deploying below Q4_K_M.
Mathematical reasoning and code generation show more sensitivity to precision than open-ended dialogue.
Calibration data quality affects PTQ results; a miscalibrated model may show unexpected regressions on domain-specific inputs.
Kernel support varies by hardware—INT4 matmul kernels are well-optimized for NVIDIA CUDA and Apple Metal but less consistent on AMD ROCm or older Vulkan back-ends.
The KV cache is typically kept at FP16 even when weights are INT4, so extremely long contexts still consume substantial memory.

The quantization process converts floating-point weights to integers using a scale-and-zero-point scheme applied over blocks of weights rather than the whole tensor. Block-wise quantization (as used in GGUF's K-quants) groups 32 or 256 weights together, computes a local scale and minimum, and stores those alongside the quantized block. This limits the error introduced by outlier weights that would force an unfavorably large global scale.
) is more accurate for weights with skewed distributions.
GGUF files use a naming scheme like
that encodes the quantization configuration. The first number (
) is the primary bit-width for most weight tensors.
means K-quant (block-wise with super-blocks and nested scales). The suffix—
—indicates how the scale tensors themselves are quantized (Small, Medium, Large), trading a small amount of file size for accuracy.
is the community default for a balanced quality-to-size ratio.

GPTQ uses second-order gradient information (the Hessian of the layer's output) to minimize quantization error layer by layer, applying weight updates to compensate for rounding. AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantization) identifies the 1% of weights that are most salient to activations and protects them at higher precision, reducing perplexity loss significantly compared to naive round-to-nearest. Both are GPU-native formats consumed by vLLM and TGI rather than llama.cpp.

The diagram below shows the full quantization pipeline from original FP16 weights through calibration and quantization to the deployable quantized model, together with the bit-width spectrum for a 70B model.
Quantization is appropriate in nearly every local deployment scenario. The decision is not whether to quantize, but which format to choose.
when the task demands maximum accuracy (legal, medical, complex multi-step reasoning) and VRAM budget allows.
as the default for most agentic applications—it balances quality and throughput well and is the most widely tested configuration.
only when VRAM is severely constrained and quality testing confirms the task is tolerant (e.g., simple retrieval-augmented question answering).
Do not quantize the model further yourself
if a pre-quantized GGUF or AWQ variant is available on Hugging Face—the community-quantized versions are typically better calibrated.
Avoid quantization when running evaluation baselines
to compare model quality; always benchmark against FP16 or BF16.

The fastest path is to pull a pre-quantized model via Ollama, which handles GGUF download and daemon management automatically. For GPU-native serving with vLLM, AWQ-quantized models are the preferred format. For custom quantization, llama.cpp includes a

# ── Step 1: pull a GGUF Q4_K_M model via Ollama ─────────────────────────────
ollama pull llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M
# Ollama stores GGUF files under ~/.ollama/models/

# ── Step 2: verify the model is registered ────────────────────────────────────
ollama list
# NAME                            ID              SIZE    MODIFIED
# llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M    ...             4.7 GB  Just now

# ── Step 3: check VRAM before and after loading ───────────────────────────────
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.used,memory.free --format=csv,noheader
# After ollama run, llama.cpp claims ~5 GB for the 8B Q4_K_M model

# ── Step 4: Convert a local FP16 safetensors model to GGUF (advanced) ─────────
# Requires the llama.cpp repo cloned locally
pip install -r llama.cpp/requirements.txt
python llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py \
  ./mistral-7b-instruct-v0.3 \
  --outtype f16 \
  --outfile mistral-7b-f16.gguf

# ── Step 5: Quantize the F16 GGUF to Q4_K_M ──────────────────────────────────
./llama.cpp/build/bin/llama-quantize \
  mistral-7b-f16.gguf \
  mistral-7b-q4_K_M.gguf \
  Q4_K_M

"""Call an Ollama-served quantized model via the OpenAI-compatible API."""
from openai import OpenAI
import time

client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",
    api_key="ollama",  # any non-empty string
)

def benchmark_quant(model: str, prompt: str, n_runs: int = 3) -> dict:
    """Measure tokens/sec for a given model tag."""
    timings = []
    for _ in range(n_runs):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        resp = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            max_tokens=200,
        )
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        tokens = resp.usage.completion_tokens
        timings.append(tokens / elapsed)
    avg_tps = sum(timings) / len(timings)
    return {"model": model, "avg_tokens_per_sec": round(avg_tps, 1)}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_prompt = "Explain the difference between FP16 and INT4 in one paragraph."
    for tag in [
        "llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M",
        "llama3.1:8b-instruct-q8_0",
    ]:
        result = benchmark_quant(tag, test_prompt)
        print(f"{result['model']:45s}  {result['avg_tokens_per_sec']:6.1f} tok/s")
Example 1: Simple chat with a quantized model

"""Minimal example: query a Q4_K_M model running via Ollama."""
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1", api_key="ollama")

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise technical assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is INT4 quantization in one sentence?"},
    ],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
Example 2: Loading an AWQ model with vLLM

"""Serve an AWQ-quantized model with vLLM and call it via the OpenAI client."""
# First, start the vLLM server (run in a separate terminal):
#   python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
#     --model casperhansen/mistral-7b-instruct-v0.1-awq \
#     --quantization awq \
#     --port 8000

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="EMPTY")

def agent_step(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Single agent turn using the AWQ-quantized model."""
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="casperhansen/mistral-7b-instruct-v0.1-awq",
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0.0,
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content


if __name__ == "__main__":
    history = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful coding assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python function to reverse a string."},
    ]
    reply = agent_step(history)
    print(reply)
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": "Now add a type annotation."})
    print(agent_step(history))

A product team needs to build a document-analysis agent that processes confidential financial contracts. The regulatory constraint is that no document content may leave the company's private network. The team selects Mistral-7B-Instruct and benchmarks three quantization levels against a set of 50 extraction tasks (entity extraction, clause classification, numerical extraction).

On an NVIDIA A10G (24 GB VRAM), Q4_K_M achieves 42 tokens/sec while Q8_0 achieves 28 tokens/sec. Task accuracy (F1 on extraction) drops from 0.91 (Q8_0) to 0.89 (Q4_K_M)—a 2-point difference deemed acceptable for the throughput gain. The team ships Q4_K_M via Ollama with a FastAPI wrapper that batches requests from multiple agent workers. The total cost is one A10G instance at approximately $0.80/hour versus cloud API costs that would run $8–12/hour at the same throughput.
The operational setup pins the Ollama version and model tag together in a Docker Compose file, ensuring reproducibility across dev, staging, and production environments.

# docker-compose.yml — pin Ollama + model tag for reproducible deployment
services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:0.3.12
    runtime: nvidia
    environment:
      - NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all
    volumes:
      - ollama_data:/root/.ollama
    ports:
      - "11434:11434"
    # Pull the model on first startup
    entrypoint: >
      /bin/sh -c
      "ollama serve &
       sleep 5 &&
       ollama pull mistral:7b-instruct-q4_K_M &&
       wait"

volumes:
  ollama_data:

— teams often pull Q4 models and ship without checking agent-specific tasks like tool-call accuracy or structured output parsing. A 2–3% perplexity increase can cause disproportionate drops in JSON validity rates.
Confusing model size with quantization level
— a Q4 8B model uses less memory than a Q8 7B model; always compute the actual byte count: parameters × bits ÷ 8.
Not accounting for KV cache overhead

— the KV cache grows linearly with context length and batch size, and is usually kept in FP16. A 70B Q4 model uses 38 GB for weights but the KV cache can add another 10–20 GB for long-context multi-turn conversations.
— running different quant levels in dev vs. production makes regressions hard to attribute. Pin the quant tag in version control.
Quantizing models that already have LoRA adapters loaded
— LoRA adapters operate in FP16/BF16; quantizing the base model after merging adapters is correct, but quantizing before merging loses adapter precision.
Always run agent-specific eval after choosing a quant level—perplexity is a proxy, not a direct measure of task accuracy for structured outputs or tool calls.
Start with Q4_K_M as the default and move up (Q6_K, Q8_0) only if quality benchmarks demand it, or down (Q3_K_M) only if VRAM is exhausted and quality tests pass.
Use pre-quantized models from Hugging Face (TheBloke, bartowski, etc.) rather than quantizing FP16 models yourself—community quants are calibrated on diverse datasets and widely tested.
Record the quantization format, model tag, and engine version as a versioned artifact in your model registry alongside the evaluation scores.
For GPU serving, prefer AWQ or GPTQ formats with vLLM over GGUF—they use CUDA kernels tuned for NVIDIA hardware and support continuous batching natively.
Monitor perplexity on your domain corpus rather than standard benchmarks; a coding agent needs to be evaluated on code, not general-purpose text.

Token generation throughput in memory-bandwidth-bound regimes scales approximately linearly with the ratio of bit-widths. Moving from FP16 (16 bits) to INT4 (4 bits) gives a theoretical 4× throughput increase; in practice, dequantization overhead and kernel efficiency reduce this to roughly 2–3× on an RTX 4090. The table below shows representative throughput for a 7B parameter model on a single consumer GPU.

Throughput figures are approximate and hardware-dependent. Compute-bound scenarios (prefix fill, prompt processing) benefit less from weight quantization than memory-bandwidth-bound scenarios (single-stream token generation), so the speedup is most pronounced for long output sequences.

For multi-user serving, vLLM's continuous batching benefits significantly from quantization because more parallel requests can share GPU memory. Scaling from 10 to 30 concurrent agent sessions on a fixed GPU budget often requires moving from Q8 to Q4 to keep all model copies resident.
The GGUF format (successor to GGML) is the standard for llama.cpp and Ollama. The llama.cpp repository ships a
binary that converts F16 GGUF files to any supported quantization level. GGUF files embed all tokenizer metadata, model config, and metadata alongside weights in a single file, making them portable.

library applies AWQ quantization to any Hugging Face causal LM. It produces a model directory compatible with vLLM and TGI. Calibration uses a configurable number of samples from a dataset; the default 128 C4 samples suffices for most models.

"""Apply AWQ 4-bit quantization to a Mistral 7B model.

Requires: pip install autoawq transformers
Runs on a GPU with at least 24 GB VRAM.
"""
from awq import AutoAWQForCausalLM
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

MODEL_PATH = "mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3"
OUTPUT_PATH = "./mistral-7b-instruct-awq"

# Quantization config — 4-bit, group size 128 is the AWQ default
quant_config = {
    "zero_point": True,   # asymmetric quantization
    "q_group_size": 128,  # weights quantized in groups of 128
    "w_bit": 4,
    "version": "GEMM",    # GEMM kernel (fastest on most GPUs)
}

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_PATH, trust_remote_code=True)
model = AutoAWQForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL_PATH, safetensors=True)

# Calibrate and quantize (128 samples from 'pileval', ~10-15 min on A100)
model.quantize(tokenizer, quant_config=quant_config)

# Save the quantized model and tokenizer
model.save_quantized(OUTPUT_PATH)
tokenizer.save_pretrained(OUTPUT_PATH)
print(f"Saved AWQ model to {OUTPUT_PATH}")
library provides NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat) and INT8 quantization that loads the model directly via Hugging Face's
arguments. It is designed for fine-tuning with QLoRA rather than pure inference, but works well for evaluation and development.
Quantization primarily affects output quality, but there are operational security and reliability considerations when deploying quantized models.
— always check SHA-256 checksums of downloaded GGUF files against the Hugging Face repository. Tampered model weights can produce subtly incorrect outputs that are hard to detect at runtime.

— aggressive quantization may erode the instruction-following and safety fine-tuning of aligned models, potentially increasing refusal bypass rates. Test alignment-critical behaviors after quantizing.

— quantized models with temperature=0 are deterministic on the same hardware and kernel version. Cross-hardware determinism is not guaranteed; different dequantization kernel implementations can produce slightly different floating-point results.

— the KV cache grows with concurrent sessions. Overloaded servers will throw out-of-memory errors unless the serving layer (vLLM, TGI) enforces a maximum batch size. Set explicit VRAM limits via engine configuration.

Think of quantization like compressing an audio recording from 24-bit studio quality (FP32) to 8-bit audio (INT8) or even 4-bit (INT4). The waveform is still recognizable and the listener can follow the melody and lyrics, but fine details—subtle harmonics, quiet background instruments—are lost. For most listening contexts the compressed version is perfectly usable; for critical studio mixing it is not. Similarly, quantized LLMs retain the model's broad linguistic capabilities and reasoning, but precision on fine-grained structured outputs or domain-specific edge cases can degrade.

Block-wise quantization adds another analogy layer: instead of compressing the entire recording with one global volume scale, you compress each 10-second segment independently, choosing the scale that best represents that segment's dynamic range. This prevents a single loud moment from forcing all quiet moments into silence—the same principle limits how far outlier weights can distort the compression of their neighbors.
Quantization reduces model weight precision (FP16 to INT4/INT8) to shrink memory footprint and increase tokens per second, with modest quality trade-offs at Q4_K_M and higher.
Block-wise quantization (GGUF K-quants) applies local scale factors per group of weights, limiting the impact of outlier weights compared to global quantization.
Q4_K_M is the community default for a balanced quality-to-VRAM ratio; Q6_K and Q8_0 offer higher accuracy at the cost of more memory; Q3 and Q2 should only be used when VRAM is severely constrained.
Use GGUF/llama.cpp for CPU-capable and Apple Silicon deployments; use AWQ/GPTQ with vLLM or TGI for high-throughput NVIDIA GPU serving.
Always evaluate agent-specific tasks—tool-call accuracy, JSON validity, structured output parsing—after changing the quantization level; aggregate perplexity is not sufficient.
The KV cache stays in FP16 regardless of weight quantization, so long-context and high-concurrency scenarios still require substantial VRAM beyond the weight footprint alone.
Pin the quantization format, model tag, and engine version together in deployment manifests to ensure reproducible quality across environments.
